elements.sent-2411-001: In this work, we theoretically study temporal interfaces between media with strong spatial dispersion and dielectrics.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we consider a temporal discontinuity that transforms a wire medium sample -- a metamaterial with resonant spatial dispersion -- into a uniaxial dielectric.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that this transition results in a transformation of the deeply subwavelength spatial spectrum of TEM waves propagating in the wire medium at a certain frequency into a spectrum of plane waves at new frequencies that are all higher than the initial one.
elements.sent-2411-004: The waves at different frequencies propagate in different directions.
elements.sent-2411-005: Their complex amplitudes and propagation directions are uniquely related to the amplitudes of the spatial harmonics of the fields which existed before the transition.
elements.sent-2411-006: We explain how to implement this transition.
elements.sent-2411-007: The revealed effect may result in a promising method of subwavelength imaging.
elements.sent-2411-008: In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in analyzing electromagnetic time-varying systems, e.g.~\cite{galiffi2022photonics,ptitcyn2023tutorial , particularly focusing on temporal discontinuities~\cite{mostafa2024temporal .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This research domain considers sudden changes at specific moments of time in effective parameters that characterize the system response.
elements.sent-2411-010: In the realm of electromagnetic media, these abrupt jumps, such as in the permittivity tensor, give rise to time interfaces between different media.
elements.sent-2411-011: Considering such interfaces in complex media results in uncovering a multitude of functionalities including polarization conversion~\cite{xu2021complete , polarization-dependent analog computing~\cite{RizzaCastaldiGaldi23 , polarization splitting~\cite{mostafa2023spin , polarization rotation~\cite{mirmoosa2024time , inverse prism~\cite{akbarzadeh2018inverse , anti-reflection temporal coatings~\cite{ramaccia2020light,pacheco2020anti , temporal aiming~\cite{pacheco2020aiming , wave freezing and thawing~\cite{wang2023controlling , wiggler-mode production~\cite{jiang1975wave, kalluri1988reflection, Wilks1988TH , a transformation of surface waves into free-space radiation~\cite{Grap19SPP,wang2023controlling , direction-dependent wave manipulation~\cite{mirmoosa2024time , and so forth.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Despite the extensive exploration in this field, investigations into time interfaces of media with strong spatial dispersion remain conspicuously absent.
elements.sent-2411-013: One specific example of those media is a metamaterial of thin parallel wires called wire medium (WM) {whose electromagnetic properties and prospective applications were thoroughly reviewed in~\cite{simovski2012wire .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Among these applications, the most relevant for the present paper is the radio-frequency, microwave, THz and infrared endoscopy which grants subwavelength resolution of imaging devices.
elements.sent-2411-015: The subwavelength resolution of corresponding WM endoscopes was experimentally demonstrated in papers \cite{Slobo,belov2005canalization,belov2008transmission,tuniz2013metamaterial,casse2010super and several further works.}
elements.sent-2411-016: After publication of the overview~\cite{simovski2012wire some new applications of WM were found, such as radiative cooling studied in works \cite{hypersim,Kos1,Kos2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This discontinuity transforms the strongly spatially dispersive WM of continuous wires into an artificial uniaxial dielectric with negligible spatial dispersion.
elements.sent-2411-018: At this stage, we analyze a temporal discontinuity in an infinitely extended WM sample.
elements.sent-2411-019: We deduce the amplitudes of the time-refracted and time-reflected waves at new frequencies and analytically show that at such temporal interfaces, conversion of subwavelength field distributions into propagating waves takes place.
elements.sent-2411-020: Due to this effect, the spatial variations of the field distribution that exist before the parameter jump are ``encoded'' in both the frequency spectrum and directivity pattern of emitted radiation.
elements.sent-2411-021: Based on the theoretical results, we show that an implementation of such temporal discontinuities grants a promising method of subwavelength imaging at THz frequencies and propose an alternative approach to subwavelength imaging using switchable wire-medium endoscopes.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this scenario, there is no need for an electrical connection of the WM sensor with the image acquisition device.
elements.sent-2411-023: The image can be restored at a distance by a spectroscopic measurement of the angular pattern produced by the WM sensor after the temporal jump.
elements.sent-2411-024: In a wire medium (WM) of continuous perfectly conducting wires, eigenmodes at frequencies below the plasma frequency $\omega_p$ are transverse electromagnetic (TEM) waves propagating along the wires without dispersion.
elements.sent-2411-025: Both phase and group velocities along the wires are equal to $c/n$, where $c=1/\sqrt{\varepsilon_0\mu_0}$ is the speed of light in vacuum, and $n=\sqrt{\varepsilon_h}$ is the refractive index of the host medium \cite{belov2003strong .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Meanwhile, the spatial frequencies, i.e., the transverse wavenumbers of these waves occupy an ultra-broad band from zero to $\pi/a$, where $a$ is the period of the WM.
elements.sent-2411-027: The waves with larger transverse wavenumber cannot propagate along the wires at the frequency $\omega_1$ which is smaller than the effective plasma frequency $\omega_p$ \cite{maslovski2002wire .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Meanwhile, the wavevector $\_k_1$ comprises both $x$- and $y$-components and may be tilted with respect to the wires under the nonzero angle $\pi/2-\psi$, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In accordance with \cite{belov2003strong , a lossless WM is described by the spatially dispersive permittivity tensor with the axial component \e \varepsilon^{(1) _{yy =\varepsilon_{h}\left(1-{k_p^2\over k_{10 ^2-k_{1y ^2}\right).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The spatial dispersion is manifested in Eq.~\r{eps by the dependence of the axial permittivity on the wavevector component $\_y_0k_{1y}$ {(here and below $\_y_0$, as well as $\_x_0$ and $\_z_0$, denote the unit vectors of the Cartesian coordinate axes) .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: If the wires are thin enough, i.e.~$r_0\le 0.1a$ (see, e.g.,~Ref.~\cite{maslovski2002wire ), the transverse component $\varepsilon^{(1) _{xx =\varepsilon^{(1) _{zz}$ of the permittivity tensor is practically not affected by the wires and approximately equals $\varepsilon_h$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Here, we orient the axis $z$ so that there is no field variation along it and consider the case when the magnetic field $\_H_1$ is polarized along $z$.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=14cm]{fig1.png} \caption{(a) Wire medium of solid wires in which a non-uniform TEM wave with the {nonzero} spatial frequency $k_{1x}$ propagates along $y$. The wavevector $\_k_1$ describes the phase velocity tilted to the wires (bottom inset). Continuous wires can be obtained from split wires using, for example, microplasma gas discharge switches (top inset). (b) Wire medium of split wires in which the time-refracted and the time-reflected TM-polarized waves arise. This medium is a uniaxial lattice of electric dipoles (top inset). On the bottom inset the vectors of the time-refracted and time-reflected waves arising in this medium are shown.} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-033: In Fig.~\ref{fig1 (a), the spatial frequency is shown by a dashed arrow to stress that this value describes only the phase distribution across the energy propagation direction.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Since the phase front of the TEM wave is tilted by the angle $\pi/2-\psi$ to the plane $(x-z)$, the vector $\_k_1$ corresponds to the phase velocity $\omega_1/k_1=c\sin\psi/n$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The electric and magnetic field vectors are, respectively, $\_E_1=E_1\_x_0$, $\_H_1=H_1\_z_0=E_1\_z_0/\eta$, where $\eta=\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_h}$ is the wave impedance of the host medium \cite{belov2003strong .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Further, for simplicity of writing, we assume that the host medium of our wires is air ($\varepsilon_h=1$).
elements.sent-2411-037: Let at the moment $t=0$, simultaneous breaks occur in every wire at a sufficiently small period $d\ll\lambda_1$, where $\lambda_1=2\pi c /\omega_1$ (recall that this wavelength is much larger than $a$ since $\omega_1\ll \omega_p$).
elements.sent-2411-038: Consequently, the WM of solid wires (WM~1) transforms into a WM of split wires (WM~2).
elements.sent-2411-039: In this transition, waves at a new frequency $\omega_2$ arise in accordance with the general theory of temporal discontinuities in materials~\cite{morgenthaler1958velocity .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Assume that the splits are of substantial length ($\Delta\gg r_0$, where $\Delta$ is the split width and $r_0$ is the wire radius) and that the period $d$ remains subwavelength also at $\omega_2$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Hence, even in the case $\Delta\ll d$, WM~2 will be a regular metamaterial of short metallic rods of length $l=d-\Delta$, and its permittivity can be found as~\cite{collin1990field : \e \varepsilon^{(2) _{yy =1+{\alpha/V\over 1-\alpha C , \l{exp}\f where $V=da^2$ is the lattice unit cell volume, $C=1.2/(\pi d^3)-(8\pi/d^3)K_0(2\pi a/ d)$ is the lattice interaction factor ($K_0$ is the McDonald function), and $\alpha$ is the polarizability of the rod of length $l$ that is defined in \cite{collin1990field as $\alpha\equiv p/\varepsilon_0E_{\rm ly}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Here, $p=p_y$ is the rod dipole moment, $E_{\rm ly}$ is the $y$-component of the local electric field at the rod center.
elements.sent-2411-043: If $a=d$, the formula for $C$ gives $C=1/3$, and the expression for $\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy}$ transforms into the well-known Lorenz-Lorentz formula.}
elements.sent-2411-044: In Ref.~\cite{collin1990field , $\alpha$ is given only for spheroidal metal particles.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Formula (5.17) from~\cite{tretyakov2003analytical gives for a short thin rod $\alpha=4l^2/(3j\omega_2 \varepsilon_0 Z_{sd )$, where $Z_{sd}$ is the complex input impedance of a short dipole~\cite{balanis2016antenna .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The real part of this impedance corresponds to the imaginary part of $\alpha$, which describes the scattering by the rod.
elements.sent-2411-047: This imaginary part must be omitted in the expression for effective permittivity.
elements.sent-2411-048: An accurate model of a uniaxial lattice \cite{belov2005homogenization shows that the scattering part of the rod polarizability cancels out with the imaginary part of the interaction constant $C$ (this imaginary part is responsible for the field retardation on the scale of a unit cell).}
elements.sent-2411-049: Therefore, the effective permittivity of a lossless lattice is obviously real and in its expression we should replace the complex polarizability $\alpha$ by its real part $\alpha'$ substituting $Z_{sd =1/j\omega_2 C_{sd}$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Here, $C_{sd =\pi\varepsilon_0l/2\log{(l/r_0)}$ is the capacitance of the short dipole (see e.g. in \cite{balanis2016antenna ).}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In the expression for $\alpha'$, the frequency $\omega_2$ cancels out, and the expression \r{exp takes the form \e \varepsilon^{(2) _{yy}\approx 1+{\alpha'\over V(1-\alpha' C) , \l{eps2}\f where $\alpha'=l^3/[6\log{(l/r_0) ]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Equation~\r{eps2 means that our WM~2 is a dispersion-free uniaxial dielectric.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Let us see what happens if the WM of continuous wires transits to such a dielectric.
elements.sent-2411-054: In accordance with Ref.~\cite{morgenthaler1958velocity , at $t>0$, both time refraction and time reflection must occur: Two waves with electric field amplitudes $\_E_2^{\pm}$ and magnetic ones $\_H_2^{\pm}$ oscillating at a new frequency $\omega_2$ will propagate in opposite directions, as it is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig1 (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The time-refracted wave inherits the previous wavevector: $\_k_{2 =\_k_{1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Meanwhile, the tilt angle $\phi$ of the electric field vector $\_E_2^{+}$ of the time-refracted wave is different from $\pi/2-\psi$ since this wave is not TEM.
elements.sent-2411-057: The time-reflected wave propagates oppositely to the time-refracted one and also has the transverse magnetic (TM) polarization, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig1 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-058: To find the amplitudes of both these waves and the new frequency $\omega_2$, we may use the time boundary conditions for vectors $\_D$ and $\_B$ \cite{morgenthaler1958velocity that can be written as follows: \e D_{1x =\varepsilon_0 E_1=D_{2x =\varepsilon_0 (E_{2 ^--E_{2 ^+)\cos\phi, \l{Morg1}\f \e B_{1 =\mu_0 {E_1\over\eta =B_{2 =\mu_0{(E_{2 ^++E_{2 ^-)\cos\phi\over Z^{TM , \l{Morg2}\f where the uniaxial impedance $Z^{TM}$ relates the transverse components of the electric and magnetic fields ($E_{2x ^{\pm = \pm E_{2 ^{\pm}\sin\phi=\pm Z^{TM H_{2 ^{\pm}$).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The time boundary condition for the axial component of the electric displacement ($D_{1y =D_{2y}$), though holds, is useless because $\varepsilon_{yy ^{(1)}$ is infinite whereas the electric field component $E_{1y}$ is null.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, we do not know these currents.
elements.sent-2411-061: To measure them is hardly realistic.
elements.sent-2411-062: The theory of wire media does not establish a unique relation between them and the electromagnetic field of the TEM mode.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=16cm]{WMedium.pdf} \caption{(a) The isofrequency contour associated with the wire medium made of perfectly conducting solid wires. The red arrows show different wavevectors for waves propagating in this medium at a fixed angular frequency. The red, green, and blue signals show the transformed waves traveling at different angular frequencies and amplitudes in the wire medium made of split wires. (b) The amplitudes of the time-reflected (blue curve with markers) and time-refracted waves (dot-dash blue curve) and the ratio $\omega_2/\omega_1$ (red curve) versus the angle $\psi$.} \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-063: From Maxwell's equations for $t>0$ with the substitution $D_{2y /\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy =\varepsilon_0(E_{2 ^- - E_{2 ^+)\sin\phi$, we obtain $\cot{\phi =\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy}\tan \psi$, $Z^{TM =\eta(1-{k_{2x ^2/ k_{20 ^2\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy )^{1/2}$, and $k_{2x E_{2y ^{\pm -k_{2y E_{2x ^{\pm =\eta k_{20 H_{2 ^{\pm}$, where it is denoted $k_{20}\equiv \omega_2/c$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Using the continuity of the axial and transverse wavenumbers $k_{2y =k_{1y =k_{10}$, $k_{2x =k_{1x}\equiv k_{10}\cot{\psi}$, we can write the dispersion equation of the disconnected WM in the form $k_{2x ^2/\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy +{k_{2y ^2 =k_{10 ^2(1+\cot^2\psi/\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy )= k_{20 ^2$, which results in the new frequency (recall that $k_{10 =\omega_1/c$ and $k_{20 =\omega_2/c$) \e \omega_2=\omega_1/\cos\phi=\omega_1\sqrt{1+{\cot^2\psi/\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The wave impedance in WM~2 takes the form $Z^{TM =\eta/\sqrt{1+\cot^2\psi/\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: By using this expression with relations \r{Morg1 and \r{Morg2 , we obtain after some algebra the amplitudes of both time-refracted and time-reflected waves: \e E_2^{\pm =\pm{E_1\over 2}\left(1\pm{\omega_2\over \omega_{1 }\right).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-068: The new frequency $\omega_{2}$ is very high and nearly equals $\omega_{1 [\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy ]^{-1/2 (k_1a)^{-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: If the spatial frequency is zero, i.e., the initial wave is a uniform plane wave propagating along the wires, it cannot be affected by this temporal discontinuity.

elements.sent-2411-071: For the latter, we employed typical parameters of a wire medium operating in the millimeter wave range.
elements.section-2411-003: Time jumps in inductively loaded wire media
elements.sent-2411-072: Now let us discuss how to transform a continuous WM into a cut-wire lattice in practice.
elements.sent-2411-073: Such transformation between arrays of continuous wires and arrays of disconnected pieces of wires can be done using some kind of switches periodically inserted into the wires.
elements.sent-2411-074: To account for finite impedances of the switches, we use {the model of a periodically loaded WM that was developed in \cite{maslovski2009nonlocal .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: If the distance between insertions is electrically small, the periodical loading of wires with the lumped impedance $Z_{\rm load}$ and the subwavelength period $d$ can be considered as an effectively uniform impedance loading.
elements.sent-2411-076: The impedance per unit length $Z_w=Z_{\rm load /d$ should be added to the wire resistance p.u.l.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Then, the axial permittivity of WM~2 becomes \cite{maslovski2009nonlocal : \e \varepsilon^{(2) _{yy =1-{k_p^2\over k_{10 ^2-j\xi k_{10 - k_{1y ^2 , \l{eps22}\f where (in the case of perfectly electrically conducting rods) the factor $\xi$ arises due to the loads: \e \xi={\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0 Z_w\over L_{WM , \quad L_{WM ={\mu_0\over 2\pi \log\left[{a^2\over 4r_0(a-r_0)}\right].}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: At a first glance, for splitting wires in WM~1 one may use such electronic switches as pin-diodes, varactors, or MEMS periodically inserted in small gaps in the wires.
elements.sent-2411-079: In the closed state they usually have some parasitic capacitive impedance $Z_{\rm load}$ which can be negligibly small compared to $\omega_1L_{WM}$, and in the open state they have a large $Z_{\rm load}$.
elements.sent-2411-080: However, our analysis has shown that the open-state impedance is not large enough.
elements.sent-2411-081: In the switched-off state, all these switches have a non-negligible capacitance, so that the ends of the cut pieces of the wires are coupled much stronger than the via free-space gaps.
elements.sent-2411-082: Let our WM~1 be loaded by inductive loads of finite length.
elements.sent-2411-083: If we remove these connecting loads, the WM~2 will be the needed array of dipole scatterers, since there will be enough large gaps between wire sections.
elements.sent-2411-084: The inductance of these connectors will add to the effective inductance of WM~1, but qualitatively will change nothing.
elements.sent-2411-085: A suitable tool that can enable this approach is gas discharge.
elements.sent-2411-086: A usual discharge such as the static arc is difficult to use in practice as it requires high voltages applied along the wires.
elements.sent-2411-087: However, an electromagnetically assisted discharge across a tiny gap which is utilized in some nanotechnologies \cite{mariotti2010microplasmas appears to be suitable.}
elements.sent-2411-088: If the wire has the thickness of a few microns and the gap $\Delta$ has the width in the interval $\Delta=10-100\, \mu$m, glow microplasma is formed in the gap under practically realistic voltages (dozens of V) and realistic microwave pumping (few mW per one gap) \cite{mariotti2010microplasmas .}
elements.sent-2411-089: In this scenario, every wire of WM~1 should be an array of metal rods whose ends are connected by microplasma channels, as it is sketched in the inset of Fig.~\ref{fig1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-090: The impedance properties of the microplasma in such channels were studied in \cite{chen2002impedance for sub-GHz frequencies.}
elements.sent-2411-091: In \cite{gautam2021high , the impedance of a microplasma capacitor with a large (1~mm) gap between the electrodes was studied in the range $0.3-20$~GHz.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Both these studies showed that the channel resistance per unit length drops versus frequency, and at microwaves it is negligibly small compared to the channel inductive impedance.
elements.sent-2411-093: Since the last one grows versus frequency linearly up to approximately 20~GHz, one may assume that the channel inductance weakly depends on the frequency also in the THz range, at least in its low-frequency part.
elements.sent-2411-094: To our estimations, the microplasma discharge of length 20~$\mu$m has the inductive impedance of the order of $|Z_{\rm load |=\omega L_{\rm channel}\sim 2$~Ohm at 100~GHz.}
elements.sent-2411-095: In accordance with \cite{maslovski2009nonlocal , we have $Z_{\rm load =j\omega_1 L_{\rm load}$ and the impedance per unit length $Z_w=j\omega_1 L_{\rm load /d$ arises in WM~1, whose axial permittivity becomes equal to \e \varepsilon^{(1) _{yy =1-{k_p^2\over k_{10 ^2-j\xi_1 k_{10 - k_{1y ^2 , \l{eps22}\f where the factor $\xi_1$ is now equal to: \e \xi_1=j{k_{10 L_{\rm load}\over d L_{WM .}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: It makes the derivations more cumbersome, but the results \r{omega2 and \r{final remain valid with a minor change: the multiplicative factor $\sqrt{1+\beta}$ arises in their right-hand sides.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: This constant factor changes qualitatively nothing in the curves depicted in Fig. \ref{fig2 (b).}
elements.section-2411-004: Application of the revealed effect for subwavelength imaging
elements.sent-2411-098: Let us discuss an interesting implication of {the revealed phenomenon.}
elements.sent-2411-099: It is known that the conversion of subwavelength field distributions into propagating modes that carry the information on the subwavelength features of the input package of evanescent spatial harrmonics can enable hyperlensing \cite{Narimanov,Shalaev,hyperreview,hypersim .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Realizations of hyperlenses are based on the use of complex materials with hyperbolic dispersion or tapered endoscopes -- wire-medium filled cables with gradually increasing distances between the wires.
elements.sent-2411-101: These endoscopes are used for THz subwavelength imaging, and their resolution and maximal length are severely restricted by losses.
elements.sent-2411-102: In this study, we show a way to a much more efficient conversion of the evanescent waves into propagating modes using time jumps of spatially uniform wire media.
elements.sent-2411-103: Indeed, in accordance with Eq.~\r{omega2 , the waves propagating in WM~1 with large spatial frequencies corresponding to the condition $k^2_{1x}\gg k_{10 ^2\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy}$ transform into plane waves propagating in WM~2 with the frequencies $\omega_2\approx \left(c/\sqrt{\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy }\right)k_{1x}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: So, new frequencies $\omega_2$ of waves that arise in WM~2 are not only uniformly proportional to spatial frequencies $k_{1x}$ but, for large values of $\psi$ correspond to new frequencies $\omega_2\sim k_{1x}$.
elements.sent-2411-105: Imagine that a complex source (also called object) with strongly subwavelength details produces electromagnetic fields in front of the WM~1 sample.
elements.sent-2411-106: These subwavelength details will produce subwavelength oscillations in waves propagating along the wires of WM~1, and high spatial frequencies $k_{1x >k_{10}$ will carry out these oscillations so that the spatial spectrum of the propagating pulse repeats the spatial spectrum of the object field.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Here, we should recall the story of far-field subwavelength imaging with WM which started from work~\cite{belov2005canalization .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Conceptual microwave studies (e.g.,~Refs.~\cite{belov2008transmission, zhao2010magnification ) stimulated the development of wire medium endoscopes {offering also imaging with strongly subwavelength resolution~\cite{tuniz2013metamaterial, brownless2015guided, casse2010super, zhou2010plasmon besides the usual THz sensing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In these endoscopes, subwavelength details of electric field distributions can be replicated at electrically long distances using plastic tubes filled with WM.
elements.sent-2411-110: The ultimate resolution of a WM endoscope is close to the period $a$ of the WM.
elements.sent-2411-111: For existing THz endoscopes $a\sim (0.05-0.1) \lambda$ \cite{tuniz2013metamaterial but, in principle, this period can be much smaller \cite{silveirinha2008ultimate .}}
elements.sent-2411-112: Both THz and infrared WM endoscopes suffer from restrictions related to the detection of subwavelength images.
elements.sent-2411-113: To capture the image, one needs THz detectors with the size of the order of $10\, \mu$m, whereas available far-field THz detectors are much larger \cite{tuniz2013metamaterial .}
elements.sent-2411-114: Near-ﬁeld THz detecting systems, which utilize microscopic probes offering subwavelength resolution, are based on raster scanning which makes them not suitable for in-situ measurements.
elements.sent-2411-115: The same and even tighter restrictions hold for infrared WM endoscopes.
elements.sent-2411-116: In the infrared experiments \cite{casse2010super, aleshire2020far, zhou2010plasmon , subwavelength images were either recorded in a photoresist layer or measured indirectly with the use of photoluminescent markers.}
elements.sent-2411-117: For THz endoscopes dedicated to in-situ imaging, one overcame the aforementioned limitations by developing a technology of 3D tapering~\cite{tuniz2013metamaterial .}
elements.sent-2411-118: With these conical WM endoscopes, it is possible to obtain magnified field distributions that couple to propagating waves, allowing the use of conventional THz optics for image acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-119: Such an endoscope is an optically thick multiwire ``cable'' of uniformly tapered wires.
elements.sent-2411-120: To practically achieve this uniform tapering, the wires are made of a liquid metal -- indium.
elements.sent-2411-121: The replacement of copper by indium results in rather high electric losses that restrict the length of the endoscope by approximately 1~cm~\cite{tuniz2013metamaterial .}
elements.sent-2411-122: For many THz applications, this length is insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-123: Moreover, it is comparable with the diameter of the conical base, which makes such endoscopes not enough flexible.
elements.sent-2411-124: The scenario of the temporal discontinuity that we have described here is an alternative approach to subwavelength imaging enabled by a WM.
elements.sent-2411-125: In this new approach, the spatially dispersive WM disappears being substituted by a split WM which is simply a uniaxial dielectric.
elements.sent-2411-126: The result of this temporal discontinuity is an angular pattern of propagating waves, where the frequencies and intensities ``encode'' the spatial structure of the object.
elements.sent-2411-127: To restore an arbitrary wave package propagating in WM~1 at a frequency $\omega_1$ it is enough to retrieve the amplitudes $E_1$ corresponding to the angles $\psi$ under which the time-refracted and time reflected waves propagate.
elements.sent-2411-129: It means that we can find $|E_1|$ from measured amplitudes and phases of emitted time-refracted and time-reflected waves.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, measuring the phases of time-refracted and time-reflected waves is not necessary.
elements.sent-2411-131: It is so because the relations \r{final are real-valued.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Using \r{final one can find both the amplitude and phase of $E_1$, if we know the permittivity $\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: This real-valued (in the lossless approximation) permittivity can be found, e.g. detecting the polarization axis of the electric field $E_2^+$ or $E_2^-$ and using the relation ${\varepsilon^{(2) _{yy =1/\tan\phi\tan\psi$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Thus, we will be able to restore the complex spatial spectrum of the spatial pulse that propagated in WM~1 before the transition via standard spectroscopic/polarization measurements in the plane $(x-y)$.
elements.sent-2411-135: The revealed field transformation effect offers a possibility for 1D subwavelength imaging by measuring TM-polarized plane waves that arise from TEM-waves or quasi-TEM waves (in case of inductively loaded wires).
elements.sent-2411-136: The subwavelength details of the field distribution can be restored only along the axis $x$.
elements.sent-2411-137: If we rearrange the vectors $\_E_1$ and $\_H_1$ in Fig.~\ref{fig1 so that $\_E_1=\_z_0E_1$, and $\_H_1=\_x_0H_1$, the waves in WM~2 whose response is described by Eq.~\r{eps2 should be two ordinary waves.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Time boundary conditions for the wavevector \cite{morgenthaler1958velocity in this case would give the solution $\omega_2=\omega_1\sqrt{1+\cot^2{\psi }$.}}

elements.sent-2411-142: This condition cannot be satisfied with a pair of ordinary waves i.e. the components of the wave vector $k_{1y}$ and $k_{1x}$ cannot be preserved together with the vector $\_B$.
elements.sent-2411-143: It means that the model of the time interface from \cite{morgenthaler1958velocity does not hold.}
elements.sent-2411-144: The same situation occurs if the wires of WM~1 disappear at $t=0$ and WM~2 transits to free space.
elements.sent-2411-145: The reason of the failure of the Morgenthaler approach \cite{morgenthaler1958velocity for this case of the initial wave polarization is the spatial dispersion in WM~1.}
elements.sent-2411-146: In general, this approach is not valid for spatially dispersive media.
elements.sent-2411-147: The reason why for the pulse with the $x$-component of the spatial spectrum this approach fortunately turned out to be applicable is that an only pair of the time-refracted and time-reflected waves arises with an only new frequency $\omega_2$.
elements.sent-2411-148: In the dual case of the polarization, more than one new frequency arises, and we plan to study this case solving all equations in the time domain.
elements.sent-2411-149: At this stage, we did not consider the impact of the finite length of the wires and finite width of the real endoscope analyzing only a temporal discontinuity in an infinitely extended WM.
elements.sent-2411-150: The impact of the finite sizes cannot change the underlying physics of the found effect.
elements.sent-2411-151: Indeed, in future studies, these factors will be taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-152: The main goal of this paper was to initiate studies of time interfaces in spatially dispersive media and show that a temporal discontinuity in a WM opens the gate to a new spectroscopic method of 1D subwavelength imaging which can be implemented in practice using split wires initially connected by micro-discharges.
elements.sent-2411-153: This work was supported by the European Innovation Council program Pathfinder Open 2022 through the project PULSE, project number 101099313.
